title: Firecrawl, Databases, and MongoDB
objective: Set the learning scope and practical anchor.
coreClaim: Firecrawl is best understood as the web acquisition layer in an AI data pipeline, while databases own durability, retrieval, governance, and product state.
practicalTakeaway: Evaluate Firecrawl and MongoDB together as pipeline components, not substitutes.

title: Why Firecrawl Exists
objective: Explain the problem Firecrawl tries to remove.
coreClaim: AI applications need current web context, but raw web pages are noisy, dynamic, and operationally expensive to convert into useful model input.
practicalTakeaway: Use Firecrawl when web acquisition is infrastructure work, not your product differentiator.

title: Learning Goals
objective: Define what the learner should be able to do after the deck.
coreClaim: A useful Firecrawl decision requires practical API knowledge, ingestion architecture, database role separation, and strategic adoption criteria.
practicalTakeaway: Keep product, technical, and strategic questions visible at the same time.

title: The Product Surface
objective: Show the public Firecrawl UI and endpoint families.
coreClaim: Firecrawl exposes the same core ideas through product UI and APIs: search, scrape, parse, map, and crawl.
practicalTakeaway: Start by learning the endpoint choice, not every option.

title: Endpoint Map
objective: Map the main Firecrawl endpoints to jobs.
coreClaim: The endpoint decision is mostly about whether you know the URL, need discovery, need a whole corpus, or need browser interaction.
practicalTakeaway: Choose the smallest endpoint that matches the uncertainty in your source set.

title: Output Contract
objective: Explain what Firecrawl returns to downstream systems.
coreClaim: Firecrawl outputs useful acquisition artifacts such as markdown, structured JSON, screenshots, links, metadata, status codes, and errors, but downstream systems decide what becomes durable truth.
practicalTakeaway: Persist the output plus provenance, not just the text.

title: Scrape
coreClaim: `/scrape` is the controlled choice for one known URL, especially when you want markdown, JSON mode, cache controls, waits, and parser options.
practicalTakeaway: Use scrape for exact pages before reaching for crawls or agents.

title: Scrape Code
objective: Show a minimal API usage shape.
coreClaim: A small Firecrawl call can hide meaningful acquisition decisions: format, freshness, timeout, and whether LLM-based cleaning or extraction is used.
practicalTakeaway: Make options explicit so ingestion behavior is repeatable.

title: Map Before Crawl
objective: Separate URL discovery from content fetching.
coreClaim: `/map` is a low-commitment way to discover and filter candidate URLs before paying the cost of scraping or crawling full content.
practicalTakeaway: Use map to control scope, cost, and corpus quality.

title: Crawl Lifecycle
coreClaim: `/crawl` turns a site into a multi-page job with discovery rules, scrape options, polling or webhook delivery, page metadata, credits, and per-page errors.
practicalTakeaway: Design crawls as jobs with state, not as one big synchronous function call.

title: Search
objective: Explain when source discovery starts as a query.
coreClaim: `/search` is useful when the corpus starts from a topic or question rather than a known site, and it can optionally scrape search results.
practicalTakeaway: Use search to find candidates, then preserve chosen source URLs for repeatable ingestion.

title: Interact
coreClaim: When static scraping is insufficient, Firecrawl can resume a scraped browser session and interact by prompt or code before returning extracted output.
practicalTakeaway: Reserve browser interaction for pages that actually require stateful UI work.

title: Structured Extraction Choice
coreClaim: Firecrawl's current guidance is to use `/agent` for autonomous discovery, scrape JSON mode for one known page, and avoid starting new work on `/extract` unless the fit is deliberate.
practicalTakeaway: Match extraction tooling to URL uncertainty and cost tolerance.

title: Running Example
coreClaim: A practical first project is a competitor-docs assistant that ingests public docs, tracks changes, embeds chunks, and answers with source provenance.
practicalTakeaway: Anchor the architecture in one repeatable workflow.

title: Ingestion Architecture
objective: Show how Firecrawl connects to storage and retrieval.
coreClaim: The production pipeline is discover, acquire, normalize, chunk, embed, index, retrieve, answer, and observe.
practicalTakeaway: Put Firecrawl at the acquisition boundary and databases after it.

title: Database Role Map
objective: Prevent the learner from treating databases as one bucket.
coreClaim: A web-to-AI system needs distinct storage roles for source inventory, job state, raw archive, normalized documents, chunks, embeddings, retrieval, evaluation, and audit.
practicalTakeaway: Name the database role before picking a database product.

title: Job Ledger
coreClaim: Async crawls, batch scrapes, webhooks, polling, retries, costs, and per-page errors require an external job ledger if the system must be debuggable.
practicalTakeaway: Store job state independently from Firecrawl's transient API responses.

title: Raw Archive
coreClaim: Raw captured content should be versioned with content hashes, source URLs, final URLs, timestamps, and acquisition settings so downstream extraction can be replayed.
practicalTakeaway: Do not overwrite the evidence that produced an answer.

title: Normalized Documents
coreClaim: After acquisition, the application needs normalized document records that combine cleaned text, source metadata, extracted fields, lineage, freshness, and policy metadata.
practicalTakeaway: Model web content as application data, not only as prompt context.

title: Chunks and Embeddings
coreClaim: RAG adds chunk boundaries, embedding model metadata, vector fields, filterable metadata, and retrieval metrics to the acquired content model.
practicalTakeaway: Keep chunks traceable to source documents and acquisition versions.

title: Where MongoDB Fits
coreClaim: MongoDB is a strong fit when scraped data is document-shaped and the application benefits from storing metadata, chunks, vectors, filters, search, and operational state close together.
practicalTakeaway: MongoDB is strongest when retrieval is tied to rich product data and metadata filters.

title: MongoDB Document Shape
coreClaim: A MongoDB document can hold source lineage, normalized content, extracted fields, freshness metadata, and retrieval-facing chunk metadata without forcing premature relational decomposition.
practicalTakeaway: Use document structure to keep provenance beside content.

title: MongoDB Retrieval
objective: Explain vector, full-text, and hybrid retrieval.
coreClaim: MongoDB Vector Search supports embedding fields, vector indexes, ANN and ENN search, metadata pre-filtering, and hybrid search with full-text results.
practicalTakeaway: Use hybrid retrieval when exact terms and semantic similarity both matter.

title: Change Detection Loop
coreClaim: Monitoring and re-crawling turn ingestion into a delta system where pages become same, new, changed, removed, or error and downstream indexes update selectively.
practicalTakeaway: Design for deltas before the corpus becomes too large to refresh naively.

title: Quality and Evaluation
objective: Explain why parseable data is not automatically correct.
coreClaim: Structured JSON, markdown, and embeddings all need evaluation: source coverage, extraction accuracy, retrieval recall, answer citation quality, and freshness.
practicalTakeaway: Create golden source sets and measure useful answers, not just successful API calls.

title: Governance
coreClaim: Web acquisition systems need source policy, robots behavior, retention controls, credentials boundaries, and auditability in addition to crawling mechanics.
practicalTakeaway: Store source policy and retention settings as data.

title: Reliability
coreClaim: Rate limits, concurrency limits, queue waits, retryable errors, and `Retry-After` handling should be explicit parts of the ingestion runtime.
practicalTakeaway: Use backoff, idempotency, and dashboards instead of blind retries.

title: Cost Model
coreClaim: The total cost of Firecrawl-backed RAG includes discovery, scraping, browser concurrency, extraction, embedding, database storage/compute, refreshes, and evaluation.
practicalTakeaway: Optimize cost before acquisition with scope controls and after acquisition with dedupe.

title: Build, Buy, or Blend
coreClaim: Firecrawl is attractive when web acquisition is common infrastructure; custom systems are attractive when source behavior, legal constraints, or unit economics dominate.
practicalTakeaway: Decide by workload evidence, not by crawler enthusiasm.

title: Database Alternatives
objective: Position MongoDB relative to other stores.
coreClaim: MongoDB can cover document storage, metadata, vector search, and hybrid retrieval, but object stores, queues, warehouses, graph databases, and specialized vector databases may still be the right role-specific choice.
practicalTakeaway: A good architecture may use MongoDB for several roles, not necessarily every role.

title: Adoption Path
objective: Give a concrete first implementation sequence.
coreClaim: The safest adoption path is a bounded pilot with source inventory, map/crawl policy, job ledger, raw archive, normalized documents, embeddings, retrieval evals, and cost reporting.
practicalTakeaway: Pilot the whole pipeline, not only the scrape call.

title: Anti-Patterns
coreClaim: Common failures include crawling everything, skipping source provenance, embedding unfiltered boilerplate, treating JSON extraction as truth, and ignoring refresh economics.
practicalTakeaway: Most bad RAG starts as bad ingestion.

title: First Project Blueprint
objective: Translate the concepts into an actionable build.
coreClaim: A useful first build has one source domain, explicit inclusion rules, a job ledger, MongoDB document/chunk collections, vector/hybrid search, and a small answer-quality test set.
practicalTakeaway: Keep the first build narrow enough that failures are explainable.

title: Decision Checklist
coreClaim: The adoption decision should check source complexity, freshness needs, extraction quality, cost per useful document, operational recoverability, and database fit.
practicalTakeaway: Use the checklist before scaling the corpus.

title: Recap
coreClaim: Firecrawl acquires and cleans web context; databases make that context durable, queryable, observable, governable, and useful to applications.
practicalTakeaway: The winning system is a controlled ingestion pipeline, not a scrape pasted into a prompt.

title: References
objective: Provide source paths for further reading.
coreClaim: The deck is grounded in Firecrawl official docs and repository material plus MongoDB official vector search and integration documentation.
practicalTakeaway: Use primary sources to refresh API details before implementation.
